misconceptionSummary: Students see primes as random and do not understand why they matter or what makes them special.

visuals.ruleText: Every whole number greater than 1 can be built by multiplying primes
visuals.subText: Primes are the 'atoms' of multiplication — they cannot be broken down further

steps.narration: Primes are the irreducible building blocks of all whole numbers. Just as atoms combine to form molecules, primes multiply together to form every other number. A prime cannot be broken down into smaller factors — it is already at its simplest.

lines.text: Every number has a unique prime factorisation

steps.narration: Twelve breaks down into two times two times three — all primes. Thirty breaks into two times three times five. Every number has exactly one prime factorisation — a unique fingerprint made entirely of primes.

lines.text: Factorisation would no longer be unique  ✗
So 1 is excluded — to preserve unique factorisations  ✓

steps.narration: If one were a prime, you could multiply any factorisation by as many ones as you like — the factorisation would no longer be unique. Excluding one preserves the fundamental theorem of arithmetic: every number has exactly one prime factorisation.

visuals.label: Primes are the atoms of arithmetic — every number has a unique prime factorisation

steps.narration: Two times two times three, and two times three times five. Primes are not random — they are the fundamental building blocks from which all other whole numbers are constructed.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: seeing primes as a random list with no deeper meaning. Primes are the building blocks of all numbers — every whole number has a unique prime factorisation, like a numerical fingerprint.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Primes matter because every whole number is built from them. The prime factorisation of any number is unique — like a fingerprint. That is why 1 is excluded: including it would destroy that uniqueness.
